notes.note: Contra fortunam paratum armatumque: the Stoic ideal of equipping the mind against the blows of Fortune (fortuna), the power of chance over human affairs. Cicero praises Lucceius for this detachment in the depths of his own grief after Tullia's death in 45 BC.

notes.anchor: The chances of the gladiatorial ring
notes.note: Casus gladiatorii: a metaphor Lucceius had used (in a now-lost consolatory letter) for the reversals of public fortune, drawn from the lethal turns of luck in the arena. Cicero says these comparisons forbade him to despair utterly of the republic.

notes.anchor: all the limbs of the commonwealth
notes.note: Membra rei publicae: Cicero's image of the state as a body whose every part has been broken or maimed under Caesar's dictatorship. The figure of the body politic recurs throughout his political writing.

notes.anchor: we rendered to our country no less, certainly, than we owed
notes.note: A glance back at the suppression of the Catilinarian conspiracy during Cicero's consulship of 63 BC, in which Lucceius had been his "prompter" (auctor). The shared memory of that service is offered here as a consolation against present despair.
